en.extract:
The ashy woodswallow (Artamus fuscus), sometimes also called the ashy swallow-shrike, is a woodswallow which is found in south Asia. Like other woodswallows, it has a short curved bill, a short square tail and long wings. It is usually seen perched in groups, high on powerlines, tall bare trees and most often in areas with a predominance of tall palm trees.


Description

This stocky woodswallow has an ashy grey upperparts with a darker head and a narrow pale band on the rump. The underside is pinkish grey and the short slaty black tail is tipped in white. The finch-like bill is silvery. In flight the long wing looks very broad at the base giving it a very triangular outline. The first primary is very short. The legs are short and the birds usually perch on high vantage points from which they make aerial sallies. There are no geographic variations in plumage and no subspecies have been designated.
Males and females are indistinguishable in the field, however an old report suggests that the sexes differ in the colour of the inside of the mouth. Young birds appear barred on the underside.


Habitat and distribution

Woodswallows are found in a range of habitats from the plains to about 2,000 metres (6,600 ft), over cultivated areas, in forest clearings and often in areas with tall palm trees. The species is widely distributed across Bangladesh, India, Nepal, Sri Lanka, Thailand, Myanmar, Laos, Malaysia and China. They are absent in the very arid regions of western India. They have been recorded on the island of Maldives.


Behaviour and ecology

Ashy woodswallows are usually seen in small groups. Several birds may sit huddled side-by-side on the bare branches of a tall tree, sometimes preening each other. They also perch on high vantage points such as powerlines and pylons. From their perches, they make aerial sallies, flapping and gliding to capture insects in the air. Insects may be caught in the beak, transferred and held in their feet, torn up with their bill and swallowed without returning to the perch. They may also return to the perch with prey to feed and will sometimes sit on the ground and have been known to visit bird baths. Although mainly feeding on insects, they may take nectar from flowers of trees such as Erythrina. They have been recorded feeding on toxic butterflies of the family Danaiidae such as Euploea core which are avoided by other birds.
The breeding season in India is March to June, the nest is a shallow cup placed at some height such as at the base of the frond of a tall palm or a hollow atop a street lighting post. The usual clutch consists of 2–3 greenish white eggs with brown spots. Both parents take part in nest building, incubation and feeding the young. They will mob larger birds such as crows and birds of prey that approach too close to the nesting birds. The song consists of a varied combination of wheezy notes that may include imitations of the calls of other birds. The usual call is shrill nasal chewk.
They make seasonal movements, possibly in response to rainfall.
The woodswallows, Artamidae, are among the few perching birds that have special feathers called powder down that break up into fine dust that is spread by the birds onto their body when preening. Powder down is also found in the egrets. Members of the family have a brush-tipped tongue. They also have some of the thoracic vertebrae fused into a structure called the notarium.
A species of ectoparasitic birdlouse, Menacanthus elbeli, and a mite that lives inside the feather quill have been described from hosts of this species. Other organisms associated with the species include endoparasitic trematodes Plagiorchis dactylopharynx, Papillatrema echinata and Stomylotrema travassosi.


References

The '''ashy woodswallow''' ('''''Artamus fuscus'''''), sometimes also called the '''ashy swallow-shrike,''' is a [[woodswallow]] which is found in south [[Asia]]. Like other woodswallows, it has a short curved bill, a short square tail and long wings. It is usually seen perched in groups, high on powerlines, tall bare trees and most often in areas with a predominance of tall palm trees.

==Description==
[[Image:Ashy Woodswallow (Artamus fuscus) in Hyderabad, AP W IMG 7506.jpg|thumb|left|In flight, the broad base to the wings gives it a very triangular outline ([[Hyderabad, India]])]]
This stocky woodswallow has an ashy grey upperparts with a darker head and a narrow pale band on the rump. The underside is pinkish grey and the short slaty black tail is tipped in white. The finch-like bill is silvery. In flight the long wing looks very broad at the base giving it a very triangular outline. The first primary is very short. The legs are short and the birds usually perch on high vantage points from which they make [[Hawking (birds)|aerial sallies]]. There are no geographic variations in plumage and no subspecies have been designated.<ref name=pcr>{{cite book|author=Rasmussen, PC|author2=JC Anderton|name-list-style=amp| year=2005| title=Birds of South Asia. The Ripley Guide. Volume 2| page=593| publisher=Smithsonian Institution and Lynx Edicions|place= Washington DC & Barcelona}}</ref><ref name=hbk>{{cite book|author=Ali, S|author2=SD Ripley|name-list-style=amp| title=Handbook of the Birds of India and Pakistan. Volume 5| edition=2nd| pages=143–145|publisher=Oxford University Press|place=New Delhi}}</ref>

Males and females are indistinguishable in the field, however an old report suggests that the sexes differ in the colour of the inside of the mouth.<ref>{{cite book|author=Legge, WV|title=A History of the Birds of Ceylon. Volume 2|year=1880|pages=665–669|url=https://archive.org/stream/historyofbirdsof02legg#page/666/mode/1up/}}</ref> Young birds appear barred on the underside.<ref name=hbk/>

==Habitat and distribution==
[[File:Ashywood.JPG|thumb|left|Ashy woodswallows huddling]]
Woodswallows are found in a range of habitats from the plains to about {{convert|2000|m}}, over cultivated areas, in forest clearings and often in areas with tall palm trees. The species is widely distributed across [[Bangladesh]], [[India]], [[Nepal]], [[Sri Lanka]], [[Thailand]], [[Myanmar]], [[Laos]], [[Malaysia]] and [[China]]. They are absent in the very arid regions of western India.<ref name=hbk/><ref>{{cite journal|author=Stonor, CR |year=1946|title= The Ashy Swallow-Shrike (''Artamus fuscus'') in the Nilgiris |journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=46| issue=1|page=184|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48199683}}</ref> They have been recorded on the island of Maldives.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Anderson, RC |year=2007 |journal=Forktail |pages=135–144 |title=New records of birds from the Maldives |url=http://www.orientalbirdclub.org/publications/forktail/23pdfs/Anderson-Maldives.pdf |volume=23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110610204219/http://www.orientalbirdclub.org/publications/forktail/23pdfs/Anderson-Maldives.pdf |archive-date=2011-06-10 }}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==

|pop1=Indian specimens<ref name=hbk/>

Ashy woodswallows are usually seen in small groups. Several birds may sit huddled side-by-side on the bare branches of a tall tree, sometimes preening each other. They also perch on high vantage points such as powerlines and pylons. From their perches, they make aerial sallies, flapping and gliding to capture insects in the air. Insects may be caught in the beak, transferred and held in their feet, torn up with their bill and swallowed without returning to the perch. They may also return to the perch with prey to feed and will sometimes sit on the ground and have been known to visit bird baths.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Inglis, CM |year=1951| title=The Ashy Swallow-Shrike (''Artamus fuscus'' Vieillot) at a bird bath| journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=50|issue=1|page=174|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48056677}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author= Santharam, V |year=1981| title= Some interesting observations: Ashy Swallow-Shrike (Artamus fuscus) on the ground|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] | volume=21| issue=9–10| pages=13–14| url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW21#page/n136/mode/1up}}</ref> Although mainly feeding on insects, they may take nectar from flowers of trees such as ''[[Erythrina]]''. They have been recorded feeding on toxic butterflies of the family Danaiidae such as ''[[Euploea core]]'' which are avoided by other birds.<ref name=hbk/>

The breeding season in India is March to June, the nest is a shallow cup placed at some height such as at the base of the frond of a tall palm or a hollow atop a street lighting post. The usual clutch consists of 2–3 greenish white eggs with brown spots. Both parents take part in nest building, incubation and feeding the young. They will mob larger birds such as crows and birds of prey that approach too close to the nesting birds.<ref name=hbk/><ref>{{cite journal| author=Santharam, V |year=1984| title= A novel nest of the Ashy Swallow-Shrike|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] | volume=24| issue=7–8|pages=8–9| url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW24#page/n51/mode/2up}}</ref> The song consists of a varied combination of wheezy notes that may include imitations of the calls of other birds. The usual call is shrill nasal ''chewk''.<ref name=pcr/><ref name=hbk/>

They make seasonal movements, possibly in response to rainfall.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Thyagaraju, AS |year=1933| title=A note on the migration of the Swallow-Shrike ''Artamus fuscus'' (Vieil)|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume= 36|issue=4|page=996|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48291912}}</ref>

The woodswallows, Artamidae, are among the few perching birds that have special feathers called powder down that break up into fine dust that is spread by the birds onto their body when preening.<ref>{{cite journal|title=A new genus and species of Cotinga from eastern Peru|author=Lowery Jr., GH|author2=JP O'Neill|name-list-style=amp|journal=The Auk|volume=83| issue=1| year=1966| pages=1–9| url= http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v083n01/p0001-p0009.pdf|doi=10.2307/4082975|jstor=4082975}}</ref> Powder down is also found in the egrets. Members of the family have a brush-tipped tongue. They also have some of the thoracic vertebrae fused into a structure called the notarium.<ref>{{cite journal|journal=The Auk |volume=126|issue=4|pages=862–872|year=2009|doi=10.1525/auk.2009.08194|author=James, HF|title=Repeated Evolution of Fused Thoracic Vertebrae in Songbirds|s2cid=59479186|url=http://si-pddr.si.edu/jspui/bitstream/10088/8384/1/vz_James-09-Auk-notarium.pdf}}</ref>

A species of ectoparasitic birdlouse, ''Menacanthus elbeli'', and a mite that lives inside the feather quill have been described from hosts of this species. Other organisms associated with the species include endoparasitic trematodes ''Plagiorchis dactylopharynx'', ''Papillatrema echinata'' and ''Stomylotrema travassosi''.<ref>{{cite journal|title=A review of quill mites of the genus Syringophiloidus Kethley, 1970 parasitizing quills of passeriform birds, with descriptions of four new species (Acari: Prostigmata: Syringophilidae)|author=Skoracki, Maciej|journal=Genus | volume=15| issue=2| pages=281–300| year=2004|url=http://www.biol.uni.wroc.pl/cassidae/Syringophyloidus%20new.pdf}}</ref>

zh.extract:
灰燕鵙（学名：Artamus fuscus）为燕鵙科燕鵙属的鸟类，俗名灰森燕。分布于印度（不包括較沙漠化的地區）、尼泊爾、孟加拉国、斯里兰卡、缅甸、泰国、寮國、柬埔寨、馬來西亞、越南以及中国大陆的云南、广西、广东、海南等地，甚至有紀錄表示灰燕鵙也可能分布於馬爾地夫一帶，一般生活于以棕榈科为主的常绿阔叶林以及营巢于高大树干的洞穴或枝桠处。该物种的模式产地在孟加拉国。


辨識特徵

全身大致呈灰色，其中腹部的羽色較淺且稍呈藕色；尾部稍尖，乍看之下有如燕子，因而得名（雌雄差異不大，目前也沒有亞種的區別）。
右边是根據自印度採得的標本所做的測量.


参考文献
